content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : Advice Meeting Participants                    Date : May 28, 1985

                                                    M-85-141

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Mary Ann

Subject Request for Technical Assistance


        On May 23, Palmer Madden called with the following question:

            Smith and Jones put on a fundraiser for candidate X
            at Jones' house. The total cost of the fundraiser
            was $499. They split the costs. Has Smith made a
            contribution to candidate X?

        Government Code Section 82015 states "...The term "contri-
        bution" does not include a payment made by an occupant of
        a home or office for costs related to any meeting or fund-
        raising event held in the occupant's home or office if the
        costs for the meeting or fundraising event are five hundred
        dollars ($500) or less."

        What if later, Smith decides to hold a fundraiser in his
        own residence for candidate X, or helps some other person
        hold a fundraiser by splitting the costs, is Smith limited
        to $250 or will all the costs related to the fundraiser
        become a contribution???

        6/11/85
        ANSWER:     The amount Smith pays is considered a contribution
                    to candidate X.

                    If later Smith decides to hold a fundraiser in his
                    own residence for candidate X, the cite of Gov.
                    Code Section 82015 above, applies.

sections.question: Smith and Jones put on a fundraiser for candidate X at Jones' house. The total cost of the fundraiser was $499. They split the costs. Has Smith made a contribution to candidate X? What if later, Smith decides to hold a fundraiser in his own residence for candidate X, or helps some other person hold a fundraiser by splitting the costs, is Smith limited to $250 or will all the costs related to the fundraiser become a contribution???
sections.conclusion: The amount Smith pays is considered a contribution to candidate X. If later Smith decides to hold a fundraiser in his own residence for candidate X, the cite of Gov. Code Section 82015 above, applies.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether splitting fundraiser costs with another person constitutes a contribution under the $500 exemption in Government Code Section 82015, and whether the exemption applies when the same person holds multiple fundraisers.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Smith's share of the fundraiser costs constitutes a contribution to the candidate. If Smith later holds a fundraiser in his own residence, the $500 exemption under Government Code Section 82015 would apply to that separate event.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is an internal FPPC memorandum to advice meeting participants documenting a technical assistance request and response. Question and conclusion sections are present but not formally labeled with headers. Answer section appears incomplete or partially cut off.

What if later, Smith decides to hold a fundraiser in his own residence for candidate X, or helps some other person hold a fundraiser by splitting the costs, is Smith limited to $250 or will all the costs related to the fundraiser become a contribution???

CONCLUSION: The amount Smith pays is considered a contribution to candidate X. If later Smith decides to hold a fundraiser in his own residence for candidate X, the cite of Gov. Code Section 82015 above, applies.
